body: // Derive a type FROM a value so the two can NEVER drift apart.
//   typeof X   -> the value's type
//   [number]   -> union of an array's element types
//   ["field"]  -> pulls one property's type out of every element
// Reach for this when a `const` array/object is your source of truth and you want its shape as a type.
// Gotcha: the source needs `as const`, or you get widened `string` instead of the literal values.
type ${1:Name} = (typeof ${2:sourceArray})[number]["${3:field}"];$0
Derive a type FROM a value (indexed acce.description: Derive a type from a value via indexed access, so type and data can't drift. Needs `as const` on the source.

body: // Discriminated union: every variant carries a literal `kind` tag.
// TS reads that tag to know WHICH variant you have -> each variant can hold different fields.
// Reach for this when one value is one-of-several shapes, not just different data.
// Gotcha: a tag is a literal TYPE. kind: "wrapped" (quotes = this EXACT value).
//         In type space, no quotes -> `string` means ANY string; "string" means the literal word.
type ${1:Name} =
| { kind: "${2:variantA}"; ${3:fieldA}: ${4:string} }
| { kind: "${5:variantB}"; ${6:fieldB}: ${7:string} };$0
Discriminated union (literal kind tag).description: One-of-several shapes tagged by a literal `kind` so TS narrows to the right variant.

body: // `as const`    -> freeze values to their literal types (keeps "**", not widened to string)
// `satisfies T` -> a checkpoint: errors if any entry doesn't fit T, WITHOUT changing the value's type
// Reach for both when this value is your source of truth AND must obey a contract type.
// Gotcha: if you also derive a type FROM this value, keep that field loose in T (e.g. name: string),
//         or T -> derived type -> T becomes a circular reference and the whole thing breaks.
const ${1:catalog} = [
${2:/* entries */}
] as const satisfies readonly ${3:ContractType}[];$0
as const satisfies (lock + check, no wid.description: Validate a literal against a contract while keeping its narrow literal types (no widening).
